docs.title: K8sResourceResizer
docs.text:
A tool that optimizes Kubernetes resource limits and requests based on historical usage patterns.

Fork of AWS K8sResourceAutoResizer compatible with other prometheus providers and with a more modular design to support multiple strategies and easier maintenance.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: K8sResourceResizer optimizes Kubernetes resource configurations through historical usage pattern analysis. It implements Prophet, Ensemble, and Time-aware approaches to set optimal CPU and memory settings. The tool integrates with Amazon Managed Prometheus (AMP) for metrics collection and supports ArgoCD workflows. Core features include business hours awareness, trend detection, and time window analysis. Users can run it locally or integrate it into CI/CD pipelines with GitHub Actions.

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• Prophet, Ensemble, and Time-aware prediction strategies
  • Historical analysis with configurable time windows
  • Business hours awareness
  • Trend detection and analysis
  • Amazon Managed Prometheus (AMP) integration
  • ArgoCD integration for GitOps workflows

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Python 3.13 or higher
  • Access to Kubernetes cluster
  • ArgoCD for GitOps integration

docs.title: 1. Build Docker image locally
docs.text: docker build -t k8sresourceautoresizer -f Dockerfile .

docs.title: 2. Run local development environment (interactive mode)
docs.text:
Use this mode for development and testing:

docker run -it \
  --network=host \
  -v /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock \
  -v $(pwd)/kube:/root/.kube \
  -v $(pwd):/app \
  -e CLUSTER_NAME=k8s-limits-cluster \
  -e RUN_LOCAL=true \
  k8sresourceautoresizer \
  sh

docs.title: 3. Run with ensemble strategy for resource optimization
docs.text:
This mode performs actual resource optimizations:

# For local development with AWS credentials
docker run -it \
  --network=host \
  -v /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock \
  -v $(pwd)/kube:/root/.kube \
  -v $(pwd):/app \
  -e GITHUB_REPOSITORY_NAME=your-repo-name \
  -e GITHUB_USERNAME=your-gh-username \
  -e GITHUB_TOKEN=your-gh-token \
  k8sresourceautoresizer \
  sh -c "/app/k8s-limits \
    --directory /app/manifests \
    --strategy ensemble \
    --history-window 24h \
    --cpu-percentile 95.0 \
    --memory-buffer 1.15 \
    --debug"

docs.title: CI/CD Configuration
docs.text: This project provides a DOCKERFILE, that you can use to build and run it on your preferred CI/CD tool. The K8sLimitsAutoResizer docker image is meant to run on CI/CD pipelines where you have your K8s manifests and ArgoCD projects. Check the Docker, Usage, and Configuration sections below.

docs.title: GitHub Actions Setup
docs.text:
For production use, we recommend using IAM roles instead of access keys:

name: Resource Analyzer

on:
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      strategy:
        description: 'Resource recommendation strategy'
        type: choice
        options:
          - basic
          - time_aware
          - trend_aware
          - workload_aware
          - quantile_regression
          - moving_average
          - prophet
          - ensemble
        default: 'ensemble'
        required: true
      history_window:
        description: 'Historical data window (e.g., 24h, 7d, 8w, 1yr)'
        type: string
        default: '24h'
        required: true
      cpu_percentile:
        description: 'CPU percentile for recommendations'
        type: number
        default: 95.0
        required: true
      memory_buffer:
        description: 'Memory buffer multiplier (e.g., 1.15 for 15% buffer)'
        type: number
        default: 1.15
        required: true
      business_hours_start:
        description: 'Business hours start (0-23)'
        type: number
        default: 9
        required: false
      business_hours_end:
        description: 'Business hours end (0-23)'
        type: number
        default: 17
        required: false
      business_days:
        description: 'Business days (comma-separated, 0=Monday, 6=Sunday)'
        type: string
        default: '0,1,2,3,4'
        required: false
      trend_threshold:
        description: 'Trend threshold'
        type: number
        default: 0.1
        required: false
      high_variance_threshold:
        description: 'High variance threshold'
        type: number
        default: 0.5
        required: false
  schedule:
    - cron: '0 0 * * *'  # Runs daily at midnight UTC

jobs:
  analyze-resources:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      id-token: write
      contents: read

    steps:
      - name: Checkout repository
        uses: actions/checkout@de0fac2e4500dabe0009e67214ff5f5447ce83dd # v6.0.2

      - name: Run resource analyzer
        env:
          BUSINESS_HOURS_START: ${{ github.event.inputs.business_hours_start || 9 }}
          BUSINESS_HOURS_END: ${{ github.event.inputs.business_hours_end || 17 }}
          BUSINESS_DAYS: ${{ github.event.inputs.business_days || '0,1,2,3,4' }}
          TREND_THRESHOLD: ${{ github.event.inputs.trend_threshold || 0.1 }}
          HIGH_VARIANCE_THRESHOLD: ${{ github.event.inputs.high_variance_threshold || 0.5 }}
          GIT_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GIT_TOKEN }}
          GITHUB_REPOSITORY_NAME: ${{ github.event.repository.name }}
          GITHUB_USERNAME: ${{ github.repository_owner }}
        run: |
          # Create base temp directory
          TEMP_BASE=$(mktemp -d)
          mkdir -p ${TEMP_BASE}/TEMP
          echo "TEMP_BASE=${TEMP_BASE}" >> $GITHUB_ENV

          docker run --rm \
            --network=host \
            -v /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock \
            -v ${{ github.workspace }}:/app/manifests \
            -v ${TEMP_BASE}:${TEMP_BASE} \
            -e BUSINESS_HOURS_START=${BUSINESS_HOURS_START} \
            -e BUSINESS_HOURS_END=${BUSINESS_HOURS_END} \
            -e BUSINESS_DAYS=${BUSINESS_DAYS} \
            -e TREND_THRESHOLD=${TREND_THRESHOLD} \
            -e HIGH_VARIANCE_THRESHOLD=${HIGH_VARIANCE_THRESHOLD} \
            -e GIT_TOKEN=${{ secrets.GIT_TOKEN }} \
            -e GITHUB_REPOSITORY_NAME=${GITHUB_REPOSITORY_NAME} \
            -e GITHUB_USERNAME=${GITHUB_USERNAME} \
            -e TEMP_BASE=${TEMP_BASE} \
             ghcr.io/thatmlopsguy/k8sresourceautoresizer:latest \
            sh -c "/app/k8s-limits \
              --directory /app/manifests \
              --output ${TEMP_BASE}/TEMP/output.yaml \
              --strategy ${{ github.event.inputs.strategy || 'ensemble' }} \
              --history-window ${{ github.event.inputs.history_window || '24h' }} \
              --cpu-percentile ${{ github.event.inputs.cpu_percentile || 95.0 }} \
              --memory-buffer ${{ github.event.inputs.memory_buffer || 1.15 }} \
              --debug"

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
The resource optimization process follows these steps:

  1. Data Collection
  2. Connects to Prometheus Compatible data source (e.g., AMP, Prometheus, VictoriaMetrics)
  3. Queries historical CPU and memory usage metrics

  4. Collects data for specified time window (default: 24h)

  5. Resource Analysis

  6. Processes historical usage patterns
  7. Identifies deployment and container configurations

  8. Analyzes metrics using the selected strategy:

    • Basic statistical analysis
    • Time-based patterns
    • Workload characteristics
    • Growth trends
    • Seasonal variations

  9. Recommendation Generation

  10. Calculates optimal resource requests and limits
  11. Applies safety buffers and constraints
  12. Determines recommendation severity levels

  13. Generates detailed recommendations per container

  14. Manifest Processing

  15. Locates relevant Kubernetes manifests
  16. Supports both Helm charts and Kustomize configurations
  17. Identifies resource definition locations

  18. Validates manifest structures

  19. Resource Updates

  20. Updates resource configurations in manifests
  21. Maintains file formatting and structure
  22. Tracks modified deployments and files

  23. Preserves existing configurations

  24. GitOps Integration

  25. Integrates with ArgoCD for deployment
  26. Creates or updates manifests
  27. Maintains GitOps workflow

  28. Ensures proper synchronization

  29. Output Generation

  30. Creates detailed JSON report with:
    • Strategy metadata
    • Configuration details
    • Updated deployments
    • Resource recommendations

  31. Logs all actions and changes

  32. Validation & Safety

  33. Enforces minimum/maximum resource limits
  34. Validates all recommendations
  35. Maintains safety buffers
  36. Ensures backward compatibility

docs.title: Time Window Formats
docs.text: The tool supports various human-readable time formats for specifying the historical data analysis window:

docs.title: Supported Formats
docs.text:
• Hours: 24h, 12h, etc.
  • Days: 7d, 14d, etc.
  • Weeks: 1w, 2w, 8w, etc.
  • Years: 1yr, 2yr, etc.

docs.title: Common Use Cases
docs.text:
• 24h - Daily patterns (default)
  • 7d - Weekly patterns
  • 4w - Monthly patterns
  • 1yr - Yearly patterns

You can specify the time window using the --history-window flag:

k8s-limits --directory /app/manifests --strategy prophet --history-window 7d

Or via environment variable in .env:

HISTORY_WINDOW=7d

docs.title: Basic Strategy
docs.text:
Simple statistical analysis using percentiles and standard deviations of historical resource usage. Best for workloads with stable, consistent resource patterns.

  • Uses: Mean, median, and percentile calculations
  • Good for: Stable applications with predictable resource usage
  • Limitations: Doesn't account for time patterns or trends

docs.title: Time-Aware Strategy
docs.text:
Analyzes resource usage patterns based on business hours vs. non-business hours, recognizing that applications often have different resource needs during working hours.

  • Uses: Time-based segmentation of metrics
  • Good for: Applications with clear business-hour patterns
  • Considers: Defined business hours, weekdays vs. weekends
  • Limitations: May not catch seasonal or monthly patterns

docs.title: Trend-Aware Strategy
docs.text:
Identifies long-term resource usage trends, helping predict future needs based on historical growth or reduction patterns.

  • Uses: Linear regression and trend analysis
  • Good for: Applications with steady growth or seasonal patterns
  • Considers: Weekly, monthly, and quarterly trends
  • Limitations: May be sensitive to outliers

docs.title: Workload-Aware Strategy
docs.text:
Analyzes different types of workload patterns (batch jobs, web services, etc.) and their specific resource usage characteristics.

  • Uses: Pattern recognition and workload classification
  • Good for: Mixed workload environments
  • Considers: Peak usage periods, idle times, and burst patterns
  • Limitations: Requires sufficient historical data to identify patterns

docs.title: Quantile Regression Strategy
docs.text:
Advanced statistical modeling that focuses on different percentiles of resource usage, providing more nuanced recommendations.

  • Uses: Quantile regression analysis
  • Good for: Applications with variable resource usage
  • Considers: Multiple percentiles for better accuracy
  • Limitations: Computationally more intensive

docs.title: Moving Average Strategy
docs.text:
Time series analysis using different types of moving averages to smooth out short-term fluctuations.

  • Uses: Simple, weighted, and exponential moving averages
  • Good for: Noisy data with short-term variations
  • Considers: Recent trends with configurable time windows
  • Limitations: May lag behind sudden changes

docs.title: Prophet Strategy
docs.text:
Implements Facebook's Prophet forecasting tool for sophisticated time series predictions.

  • Uses: Facebook's Prophet algorithm
  • Good for: Complex seasonal patterns and holiday effects
  • Considers: Multiple seasonality, holidays, and trends
  • Limitations: Requires more computational resources

docs.title: Ensemble Strategy
docs.text:
Combines predictions from multiple strategies using weighted averaging for more robust recommendations.

  • Uses: Weighted combination of all other strategies
  • Good for: Production environments requiring high reliability
  • Considers: Confidence levels from each strategy
  • Limitations: May be more conservative in its recommendations

docs.title: Strategy Selection
docs.text:
• For simple applications: Basic or Moving Average
  • For business applications: Time-Aware or Workload-Aware
  • For growing applications: Trend-Aware
  • For complex patterns: Prophet or Ensemble

  • For maximum reliability: Ensemble

  • Automatic resource recommendations for:

  • CPU requests and limits

  • Memory requests and limits

  • Integration with:

  • Prometheus Compatible data sources (e.g., AMP, Prometheus, VictoriaMetrics)
  • Argo CD
  • Kubernetes manifests (Helm charts, Kustomize)
  • OpenAI compatible LLMs for enhanced analysis and recommendations

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
# Install basic version
uv pip install -e .

docs.title: Command Line
docs.text:
# Basic usage
uv run python -m src.main --directory /path/to/manifests

# With specific strategy
uv run python -m src.main --strategy ensemble --directory /path/to/manifests

# Debug mode
uv run python -m src.main --debug --directory /path/to/manifests

docs.title: Docker Usage
docs.text:
For Docker-based usage, refer to the Running Locally section above where detailed instructions are provided for:

  • Building the Docker image
  • Running in local development mode
  • Running with resource optimization

The container supports two main modes:

  1. Local Development (first command):
  2. Interactive mode with -it
  3. Keeps container running with RUN_LOCAL=true
  4. Mounts kubeconfig and application directory

  5. Exposes ArgoCD UI (8080) and Kubernetes API (6550) ports

  6. Resource Optimization (second command):

  7. Runs ensemble strategy for optimal resource recommendations
  8. Configurable parameters for fine-tuning recommendations
  9. Outputs results to mounted volumes

Both approaches use secure configuration:

  • No privileged mode needed
  • Docker socket mounted for DinD operations
  • Host network access for better compatibility

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
Environment variables:

  • BUSINESS_HOURS_START: Start of business hours (default: 9)
  • BUSINESS_HOURS_END: End of business hours (default: 17)
  • BUSINESS_DAYS: Business days (default: 0,1,2,3,4 where 0=Monday)
  • RUN_LOCAL: Set to "true" to keep container running for local development (default: false)
  • CLUSTER_NAME: Name of the kind cluster to create
  • GITHUB_REPOSITORY_NAME: Name of the GitHub Repository
  • GITHUB_USERNAME: GitHub user name
  • GITHUB_TOKEN: GitHub token for authentication

docs.title: License
docs.text: This library uses the MIT-0 License. See the LICENSE file.

docs.title: Docker Environment for K8s Resource Optimization
docs.text: A Docker environment for running the Kubernetes Resource Optimizer with kind, kubectl, and Argo CD. The setup runs without privileged mode for security and CI/CD platform compatibility.

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• Non-privileged mode operation
  • Pre-installed tools:
  • kind
  • kubectl
  • Argo CD CLI
  • Resource Optimization tools
  • kind cluster setup
  • Argo CD deployment
  • Local development and resource optimization modes

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Docker installed on host machine
  • Available ports:
  • 8080 (Argo CD UI)
  • 6550 (Kubernetes API)

docs.title: Building the Image
docs.text:
# Build with no cache
docker build --no-cache -t k8sresourceautoresizer -f Dockerfile .

# Or regular build
docker build -t k8sresourceautoresizer -f Dockerfile .

docs.title: Local Development Mode
docs.text:
docker run -it \
  --network=host \
  -v /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock \
  -v $(pwd)/kube:/root/.kube \
  -v $(pwd):/app \
  -e CLUSTER_NAME=eks-blog-demo \
  -e RUN_LOCAL=true \
  k8sresourceautoresizer

docs.title: Resource Optimization Mode Locally
docs.text:
docker run -it \
  --network=host \
  -v /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock \
  -v $(pwd)/kube:/root/.kube \
  -v $(pwd):/app \
  -e CLUSTER_NAME=eks-blog-demo \
  -e GITHUB_REPOSITORY_NAME=your-repo-name \
  -e GITHUB_USERNAME=your-gh-username \
  -e GITHUB_TOKEN=your-gh-token \
  k8sresourceautoresizer \
  sh -c "/app/k8s-limits \
    --directory /app/manifests \
    --strategy ensemble \
    --history-window 24h \
    --cpu-percentile 95.0 \
    --memory-buffer 1.15 \
    --debug"

docs.title: Parameters Explained
docs.text:
• --network=host: Provides host networking for better compatibility
  • -v /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock: Enables Docker-in-Docker without privileged mode
  • -p 8080:8080: Argo CD web UI access
  • -p 6550:6550: Kubernetes API server access
  • -v $(pwd)/kube:/root/.kube: Persists kubeconfig
  • -v $(pwd):/app: Mounts application directory

docs.title: Required
docs.text:
• CLUSTER_NAME: Name for the kind cluster
  • GITHUB_REPOSITORY_NAME: Name of the GitHub Repository
  • GITHUB_USERNAME: GitHub user name
  • GITHUB_TOKEN: GitHub token for authentication

docs.title: Optional
docs.text:
• RUN_LOCAL: Set to "true" for development mode
  • BUSINESS_HOURS_START: Start of business hours (default: 9)
  • BUSINESS_HOURS_END: End of business hours (default: 17)
  • BUSINESS_DAYS: Business days (default: 0,1,2,3,4 where 0=Monday)
  • TREND_THRESHOLD: Threshold for trend detection
  • HIGH_VARIANCE_THRESHOLD: Threshold for high variance detection

docs.title: Argo CD
docs.text:
• URL: https://localhost:8080
  • Default credentials will be shown in container logs
  • Login using:
argocd login localhost:8080 --username admin --password <password-from-logs> --insecure

docs.title: Kubernetes
docs.text:
# Configure kubectl
export KUBECONFIG=$(pwd)/kube/config

# Verify connection
kubectl cluster-info

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Development:
  2. Use RUN_LOCAL=true for persistent environment
  3. Mount source code for live development

  4. Keep kubeconfig persistent with volume mount

  5. Resource Optimization:

  6. Use specific time windows for analysis
  7. Start with ensemble strategy
  8. Adjust thresholds based on application patterns

  9. Monitor optimization results

  10. Use non-root user inside container when possible

  11. Keep base image updated
  12. Scan container images for vulnerabilities
  13. Use specific versions for dependencies
  14. Implement proper secret management

docs.title: Cleanup
docs.text:
# Stop container
docker stop $(docker ps -q --filter ancestor=k8sresourceautoresizer)

# Remove container
docker rm $(docker ps -aq --filter ancestor=k8sresourceautoresizer)

# Remove image (optional)
docker rmi k8sresourceautoresizer

docs.title: Docker Socket Mounting vs Privileged Mode
docs.text:
This setup uses Docker socket mounting (-v /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock) instead of privileged mode (--privileged) for enhanced security:

  1. Reduced Attack Surface: Only exposes Docker API functionality instead of full host access
  2. Principle of Least Privilege: Container only gets the permissions it needs
  3. Better Security Practices: Aligns with container security best practices
  4. Maintained Functionality: Preserves required container management capabilities

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
1. Docker Socket Permission Issues:
# Add current user to docker group
sudo usermod -aG docker $USER
# Restart Docker service
sudo systemctl restart docker
  1. Network Access Issues:
  2. Ensure host network mode is enabled
  3. Check firewall rules for required ports

  4. Verify AWS credentials have necessary permissions

  5. Volume Mount Issues:

  6. Ensure paths exist on host
  7. Check file permissions
  8. Use absolute paths when needed

For more detailed troubleshooting, check the logs with:

docker logs <container_id>

docs.title: How to create github token
docs.text:
To create a GitHub token, follow these steps:

  1. Log in to your GitHub account.
  2. Click on your profile picture in the top right corner and select "Settings" from the dropdown menu.
  3. In the left sidebar, click on "Developer settings."
  4. Click on "Personal access tokens" in the left sidebar.
  5. Click the "Generate new token" button.
  6. Give your token a descriptive name in the "Note" field (e.g., "K8sResourceResizer Token").
  7. Select the appropriate scopes for your token. For this project, you may need to select "repo" (for full control of private repositories) and "workflow" (to access GitHub Actions).
  8. Click the "Generate token" button at the bottom of the page.
  9. Copy the generated token and store it securely, as you won't be able to see it again. You will need this token to authenticate with the GitHub API when creating pull requests or performing other actions.
